items.question_text: Complete: FAA Order JO 7110.65AA, Air Traffic Control, dated _____, and all changes to it are canceled.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-4, the canceled prior edition was dated April 20, 2023. Knowing the exact date helps distinguish which edition was superseded and confirms a controller is working from the current publication.

choices.text: April 20, 2023

items.question_text: Under 1-1-4, when JO 7110.65BB was issued, the changes to JO 7110.65AA remained in effect as standalone guidance until individually reviewed and re-issued.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-4, JO 7110.65AA and all changes to it are canceled — not just the basic order. Changes do not survive independently.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order; they do not replace or override it. This single-word distinction carries the entire legal relationship between LOAs and the order.

choices.text: supplement

items.question_text: Under 1-1-10, which of the following correctly describes the relationship between an LOA and FAA Order JO 7110.65 minima?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs may not specify minima less than the order's minima unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft. The civilian exception does not exist.

choices.text: An LOA may reduce minima below the order whenever both facilities agree the reduction is safe.
An LOA may not reduce minima below the order unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft.
An LOA may reduce minima below the order for any aircraft type if the reduction is published in a NOTAM.
An LOA may always set lower minima because it is a binding agreement between facilities.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-10, which procedures or minima trigger the requirement for a letter of agreement?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, the LOA requirement is triggered when procedures or minima are applied jointly or otherwise require the cooperation or concurrence of more than one facility or organization.

choices.text: Any procedure a facility manager chooses to document, even if it involves only that facility.
Only procedures that involve military and civilian coordination.
Procedures or minima applied jointly or requiring cooperation or concurrence of more than one facility or organization.
Only procedures already approved through the waiver process under 1-1-11.

items.question_text: Complete: _____ or unusual requirements may dictate procedural deviations or supplementary procedures to this order.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-11a, waivers are justified by exceptional or unusual requirements — not routine preferences, convenience, or staffing issues. The word 'exceptional' sets the high bar for what qualifies.

choices.text: Exceptional

items.question_text: Under 1-1-11, a waiver request involves military operations. The facility supervisor obtains FAA regional approval first, then notifies the military headquarters after the waiver is approved. Is this the correct sequence?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-11b, prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters is required for subsequent interface with FAA. Military approval must come first, not after.

choices.text: No, because waivers involving military operations require prior military headquarters approval before FAA processes the waiver.
Yes, because FAA coordination must always precede military notification.
Yes, because the military notification is only for record-keeping after the waiver is approved.
No, because waivers with military involvement are prohibited entirely.

items.question_text: Complete: When references are made to non-ATO regional organizations, the facility should contact the FAA region where the facility is _____ located.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the key discriminator is physical location, not the service area office location. The word 'physically' is the entire operational rule.

choices.text: physically

items.question_text: Under 1-1-13, a facility is physically located in the Eastern region but its service area office is in the Central region. The facility needs to contact a non-ATO FAA office. Which region is correct?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the facility contacts the region where it is physically located — not the service area office region. The physical-location rule overrides any organizational alignment.

choices.text: The Central region, because that is where the service area office is located.
The Eastern region, because the facility is physically located there.
Either region, at the facility's discretion.
Neither; the facility must contact Washington headquarters directly.

items.question_text: Complete: All organizations are responsible for viewing, downloading, and _____ to receive email notifications when changes occur to this order.
items.explanation: Per 1-1-14b, subscribing is one of the three specific responsibilities organizations have. Passive awareness is not enough — the organization must actively subscribe.

choices.text: subscribing

items.situation: Two civilian facilities have been using joint arrival procedures based on a verbal understanding, and one supervisor wants to formalize the arrangement with reduced separation minima below the order's values, arguing that a signed LOA between the two facilities is sufficient to authorize the reduction.
items.question_text: Under 1-1-10, can two civilian facilities use an LOA to authorize separation minima below those in the order?

choices.text: No; LOAs only supplement the order and may not specify minima less than the order's minima except when appropriate military authority authorizes reduced separation between military aircraft.
Yes; any two facilities may agree to lower minima as long as the agreement is documented in an LOA.
Yes, but only if the LOA is also approved by the service area office.
No; LOAs may never address minima — that is reserved for waivers under 1-1-11.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order and may not specify minima less than the order's minima unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft. Civilian facilities cannot self-authorize lower minima through an LOA.

items.question_text: Under 1-1-10, where are the procedures for LOA development, review, and approval covered?

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 2, Section 1.
FAA Order JO 7210.3, Chapter 4, Section 3.
FAA Order JO 7000.5, Procedures for Submitting Changes.
FAA Order JO 1000.37, ATO Safety Management System.

items.explanation: Per the reference block in 1-1-10, LOA development, review, and approval procedures are covered in FAA Order JO 7210.3, Chapter 4, Section 3.

items.question_text: How does the approval path for a waiver differ from the approval path for a standard procedural change?

choices.text: Both follow the same path through facility management, then AJV-P correspondence.
Waivers always go directly to AJV-P; procedural changes go through facility management.
Waiver approval is governed by FAA Order JO 7210.3, Chapter 19, Section 7, which is a different order and chapter from the 1-1-8 change process.
Waivers require only local facility concurrence; procedural changes require headquarters approval.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-11, waiver approval is covered in FAA Order JO 7210.3, Chapter 19, Section 7, which is a separate order from the 1-1-8 procedural change process that goes through JO 7000.5. These are distinct administrative paths.

items.situation: An Air Force unit wants USAF-specific procedures that differ from the order for operations involving only military aircraft. The request is routed directly to AJV-P without coordination through the appropriate Air Force headquarters.
items.question_text: Under 1-1-11, is this routing correct for USAF-only procedural deviations?

choices.text: No; military operations require prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters, and for USAF procedures involving military aircraft only, HQ Air Force Flight Standards Agency is the approval authority.
Yes; the local Air Force unit can approve its own deviations without higher headquarters coordination.
Yes; as long as AJV-P receives the request, military chain-of-command review is optional.
No; only the Department of the Army can approve procedures for any military aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-11b, prior military headquarters approval is required. The Terminal note specifies HQ AFFSA as the approval authority for USAF procedures or minima that differ from the order and involve military aircraft only.

items.situation: Your facility is physically located in the Western-Pacific region but its service area office is in the Northwest Mountain region. A reference in the order requires contacting a Flight Standards regional office. A coworker suggests calling the Northwest Mountain region office because that is where the service area office sits.
items.question_text: Which region should the facility contact under 1-1-13?

choices.text: The Northwest Mountain region, because service area offices always coordinate regional matters.
The Western-Pacific region, where the facility is physically located.
Either region works; physical and service-area regions are interchangeable.
Washington headquarters, because the order does not specify which region to use.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the facility contacts the FAA region where the facility is physically located — not the region where the service area office is located. Physical location governs.
items.para_context: REFERENCES TO FAA NON-AIR TRAFFIC ORGANIZATIONS

items.question_text: Under 1-1-14a, which two FAA centers are named as specific recipients of this order's distribution?

choices.text: The William J. Hughes Technical Center and the Mike Monroney Aeronautical Center.
The William J. Hughes Technical Center and the FAA Academy.
The Mike Monroney Aeronautical Center and the Air Traffic Control System Command Center.
The FAA Technical Center and the National Aeronautical Charting Office.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-14a, the order is distributed to selected offices including the William J. Hughes Technical Center and the Mike Monroney Aeronautical Center.
